_description: Documents the explicit-tag-wins priority rule from CLASS-01. An attacker-controlled @photophore:public tag promotes tier-0 content if and only if the issuer node honors the tag — by design. Explicit tags are issuer-authored on the issuer node and are the highest-priority classification signal per CLASS-01. An attacker who can inject a forged @photophore:public tag into content has access to the issuer node, which is a different threat (AT-A1). AT-A3 is mitigated by issuer-side controls (SHADOW-04 irreversibility, audit-log surveillance, AT-A1 channel-impersonation defenses), NOT by the classifier. This fixture lives in conformance/valid/ because it documents intended behavior, not a violation. Behavioral wire: photophore/python/tests/test_classifier_priority.py.
_placement_rationale: W12: moved from invalid/ to valid/. AT-A3 documents intended behavior under CLASS-01 — explicit tag priority is spec contract, not a bug. The prior invalid/ placement implied AT-A3 was a violation; placing it here clarifies that the priority order is correct.

scenario.description: Two ContentBlocks: one with an explicit @photophore:public tag, one without.

blocks.content_summary: tier-0 content with @photophore:public tag in the body

expected_classification.note: CLASS-01 Priority 1 wins — explicit tag overrides path rule (env-credentials -> local). This is intended: the issuer node is the trust anchor.

blocks.content_summary: same tier-0 content WITHOUT the explicit tag

expected_classification.note: Without issuer-authored tag, content stays local via the env-credentials path rule.

mitigations: SHADOW-04: shadow irreversibility prevents the abstracted summary from leaking the original content
AUDIT-01..08: append-only audit log records every dispatch — surveillance catches unusual tier-escalation patterns
AT-A1: channel impersonation defenses require key_scheme to match keystore record, blocking most issuer-node compromise paths
